title: Clinical Perfusion Scientist
how_to_become: Apply for trainee opportunities on the NHS recruitment website. An honours degree in a relevant scientific or nursing subject is required along with completion of a pre-registration training programme.
description: Clinical perfusion scientists (also known as clinical perfusionists) set up and manage equipment used to support patients during cardiac surgery, isolated limb perfusion, and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation therapy.
role_overview: As a clinical perfusionist, you'll mainly work in hospital operating theatres as part of a cardiac surgical team, with some work in the intensive care unit. You'll set up and manage life-support equipment during complex surgical procedures.

entry_requirements.overview: An honours degree in a relevant scientific or nursing subject is required, plus completion of the pre-registration training programme.

work_experience: Work placements and volunteering can provide healthcare experience and help when applying to university, college, or a new job with NHSScotland.

education_and_training.training_programme: Pre-registration training programme including an MSc in Perfusion Science at the University of Bristol at SCQF level 11 and accredited work-based training.
education_and_training.qualification: Certificate of Accreditation in Basic Clinical Perfusion Sciences exam on completion.
education_and_training.registration: On passing the exam, register as a qualified clinical perfusionist with the College of Clinical Perfusion Scientists.

learning_and_development.overview: Ongoing requirements for education and skills development after registration. Clinical perfusion scientists undertake academic research as a basic part of training, which can continue after qualification.

career_progression.overview: Clinical perfusionists are highly specialised. With training and experience, they can move into teaching, management, or academic research.

professional_bodies: College of Clinical Perfusion Scientists of Great Britain and Ireland

chunks.text: To become a clinical perfusion scientist, apply for trainee opportunities on the NHS recruitment website. An honours degree in a relevant scientific or nursing subject and completion of a pre-registration training programme are required.
Clinical perfusion scientists set up and manage equipment supporting patients during cardiac surgery, isolated limb perfusion, and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) therapy.
Clinical perfusionists mainly work in hospital operating theatres as part of a cardiac surgical team, with some work in the intensive care unit.
Useful school subjects include Chemistry, English, Human Biology, Maths, and Physics. An honours degree in a relevant scientific or nursing subject is required.

Training includes an MSc in Perfusion Science at the University of Bristol at SCQF level 11 and accredited work-based training. On completion, trainees sit the Certificate of Accreditation in Basic Clinical Perfusion Sciences exam and register with the College of Clinical Perfusion Scientists.
Clinical perfusionists can progress into teaching, management, or academic research roles.
Clinical perfusion scientists register with the College of Clinical Perfusion Scientists of Great Britain and Ireland.
